original_hypothesis.title: Gas Exhaustion DoS Permanently Bricks Stale Markets via update_market Loop
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: update_market() loops from lastRewardBlock to block.number in chunks. Each iteration makes an external state-writing call to gaugeController.gauge_relative_weight_write(). If a market goes many epochs without any interaction, the loop accumulates hundreds of iterations, each with expensive external SSTORE operations. After sufficient staleness, every call to claim(), sync_ledger(), or update_market() exceeds the block gas limit, permanently bricking the market.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: With BLOCK_EPOCH=100,000 blocks (~1 week), 6 months of inactivity creates ~26 loop iterations with external storage-writing calls. The gas cost per iteration depends on gauge_relative_weight_write complexity, but at ~50-100k gas per SSTORE-heavy external call, 26+ iterations could approach block gas limits. Markets with low activity are natural targets, and the DoS is permanent with no incremental catch-up mechanism.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Unbounded while loop with external call `gaugeController.gauge_relative_weight_write(_market, epoch)` per iteration, no incremental update mechanism

preconditions: A whitelisted market exists with extended period of no interaction
Block gas limit insufficient for the accumulated loop iterations

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Loop iterations = (block.number - lastRewardBlock) / effectiveChunkSize, with external call per iteration
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Natural inactivity on low-volume markets; could be accelerated by attacker intentionally not interacting
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Griefing attack: users with deposits in the market cannot claim or withdraw. Locked rewards and potentially locked lending market funds.

economic_questions: What is the gas cost per iteration of the update_market loop on Canto?
How many epochs of staleness before the gas limit is exceeded?
Are there any markets that naturally have low activity?
validation_requirements: Calculate maximum staleness before DoS given Canto block gas limits
Test update_market with 50+ epochs of staleness

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Zero for griefing (just avoid interacting with target market)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: No direct profit; grief locks user rewards and potentially lending market funds
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Requires weeks-months of inactivity; permanent DoS once triggered

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Target low-activity or newly whitelisted markets
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Permanent loss of access to market rewards for all users

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All users with deposits in the stale market
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All rewards for the affected market since last update

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Canto block gas limit, gauge_relative_weight_write gas cost
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Simulate update_market with increasing staleness and measure gas

validation_result.mechanism: unbounded loop with external calls causes permanent DoS
validation_result.impact_summary: Markets with sufficient staleness become permanently bricked—no user can claim rewards, sync balances, or interact with the market through LendingLedger because update_market always reverts on gas exhaustion.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The update_market function contains a while loop (lines 62-72) that iterates from lastRewardBlock to block.number in chunks of at most BLOCK_EPOCH (100,000 blocks). Each iteration makes an external call to gaugeController.gauge_relative_weight_write(_market, epoch). The lastRewardBlock is only updated AFTER the entire loop completes (line 73), meaning partial progress is impossible—if the loop exceeds the block gas limit, the transaction reverts entirely and lastRewardBlock remains at its stale value. This creates a permanently worsening condition: once a market is stale enough to exceed gas limits, every subsequent attempt also fails because the staleness only increases.

The number of iterations is (block.number - lastRewardBlock) / BLOCK_EPOCH approximately. With BLOCK_EPOCH=100,000 (~1 week), 6 months of inactivity produces ~26 iterations, 1 year ~52, and so on. The actual gas threshold depends on the cost of gauge_relative_weight_write, which is an external state-writing call (implied by _write suffix). In Curve-style gauge controllers, this function itself may perform time-period checkpointing with its own loop, compounding gas costs significantly. Even without the compound effect, each iteration involves: an external CALL to gaugeController, storage reads/writes for accCantoPerShare and secRewardsPerShare (SSTORE), and arithmetic. At conservative estimates of 50-100k gas per iteration (including the external call's internal storage operations), ~300-600 iterations would exhaust a 30M gas limit. With compound checkpoint effects in the gauge controller, the threshold could be much lower.

The defect is structural: there is no maximum iteration cap, no incremental catch-up mechanism, and no way to advance lastRewardBlock without completing the entire loop. This is a textbook unbounded-loop DoS pattern.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines 58-73 of LendingLedger.sol confirm the unbounded while loop with external calls. Line 73 confirms lastRewardBlock is only set after the loop completes, making partial progress impossible. There is no MAX_ITERATIONS constant or early-exit mechanism anywhere in the code. The function is called by claim() (line 97), sync_ledger() (line 81), and directly, meaning all user-facing operations are blocked when the loop exceeds gas limits.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The while loop iterates from lastRewardBlock to block.number with no maximum iteration limit. Each iteration makes an external state-writing call to gaugeController.gauge_relative_weight_write(). The lastRewardBlock is only updated on line 73 AFTER the loop completes, so if the loop exceeds gas limits the transaction reverts entirely with no partial progress. Once a market becomes sufficiently stale, it is permanently bricked.

calling_sequence: 1. User calls claim(_market) at line 96, or lending market calls sync_ledger(_lender, _delta) at line 79
2. Both call update_market(_market) at lines 97 and 81 respectively
3. update_market enters while loop at line 62: while (i < block.number)
4. Each iteration calls gaugeController.gauge_relative_weight_write(_market, epoch) at line 69 - external state-writing call
6. If total gas exceeds block limit, transaction reverts
7. lastRewardBlock at line 73 is never reached, so staleness is unchanged
8. All subsequent calls also fail because staleness is now worse
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The critical path is the while loop at lines 62-72. The loop variable i starts at market.lastRewardBlock (line 62) and advances by blockDelta each iteration (line 71), where blockDelta is at most BLOCK_EPOCH = 100,000 (line 64-65). The external call at line 69 to gauge_relative_weight_write is made every iteration. Since lastRewardBlock is only written at line 73 (after the loop), any gas-limit revert leaves the state unchanged, creating a permanent DoS once the gap is large enough.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided. The vulnerability is structural in the contract code regardless of deployment configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack requires a whitelisted market to have no interactions (no claim, sync_ledger, or update_market calls) for a sufficiently long period. Low-activity markets or markets with small user bases are natural candidates. An attacker could deliberately avoid interacting with a market where they have deposits, allowing staleness to accumulate. Once the gas threshold is crossed, all user operations on that market are permanently bricked. The attacker doesn't need capital—just strategic inaction on a low-activity market. The exact time threshold depends on gauge_relative_weight_write gas costs, which are not available in scope, but the structural vulnerability (unbounded loop, no incremental catch-up, all-or-nothing lastRewardBlock update) is definitively present in the code.
validation_result.impact_assessment: If triggered, all users with deposits in the affected market permanently lose access to their accumulated CANTO rewards. The sync_ledger function also becomes uncallable, meaning the lending market cannot update user balances in the ledger—this could have cascading effects on the lending market itself if it relies on sync_ledger for proper operation. The condition is irreversible without contract migration or upgrade (and the contract has no upgrade mechanism). Even re-whitelisting doesn't help because it resets lastRewardBlock but only on a false->true transition, and the market would need to be de-whitelisted first, which locks rewards (INV-STATE-003).
recommended_mitigations: Add a maximum iteration limit (e.g., MAX_EPOCHS = 52) to the while loop, allowing partial catch-up: if the loop reaches the limit, update lastRewardBlock to the progress point and return
Allow update_market to be called with an explicit target block number parameter, enabling incremental catch-up across multiple transactions
Add a keeper/bot mechanism that periodically calls update_market on all whitelisted markets to prevent staleness accumulation
Consider adding an emergency governance function to manually set lastRewardBlock forward (skipping rewards) as a recovery mechanism for bricked markets
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy LendingLedger with a mock GaugeController that has realistic storage-writing behavior in gauge_relative_weight_write. 2. Whitelist a market and deposit some balance. 3. Advance block.number by varying amounts (26 epochs, 52 epochs, 100 epochs, 500 epochs). 4. Measure gas consumption of update_market at each staleness level. 5. Identify the staleness threshold where gas exceeds 30M. 6. Confirm that after crossing the threshold, all subsequent calls to claim/sync_ledger/update_market revert with out-of-gas.
uncertainties: The exact gas cost of gaugeController.gauge_relative_weight_write is unknown because the GaugeController source is not in scope. If it's very cheap (e.g., <10k gas), the DoS threshold would require many years of inactivity. If it's expensive (e.g., 100k+ gas with its own catch-up loop), the threshold could be reached in months.
Canto's block gas limit may differ from Ethereum's 30M—a higher limit would extend the safe staleness period.
In practice, any market with even one active user periodically calling claim() would be kept current, reducing the realistic attack surface to truly abandoned markets.
additional_testing_needed: Gas profiling of gauge_relative_weight_write with varying staleness periods
Confirm Canto mainnet block gas limit
Enumerate existing whitelisted markets and their activity frequency to assess real-world risk
Test whether the GaugeController's own internal checkpointing creates compound gas growth
